content.assessment: This proposal seeks to formalize and extend an existing discretionary administrative power across all maintainer types, directly confronting the jurisdictional gap that limits T2. It is legally creative in leveraging the 2023 uniform employment-status law as a possible bridge to cross-maintainer reach, but doing so for admissions/expansion gatekeeping (rather than employment terms) would likely require a distinct and untested legal extension, and raises direct autonomy conflicts with church and private maintainers.
opportunities: If successfully extended, this would close the accountability gap left by T2's state-sector-only reach, creating a single coherent authority test applicable across the whole system (F-live-legal_and_governance-07)
strengths: Builds on documented precedent of ministerial discretionary refusal on segregation grounds (Tiszavasvári), giving the proposal an existing administrative-legal foothold rather than requiring wholly new authority (F-live-legal_and_governance-07 context of existing partial jurisdiction)

The 2023 law unifying employment/legal-status terms across all provider types demonstrates that cross-maintainer legal reach has been successfully extended before, offering a plausible (though not identical) legal template (F-live-legal_and_governance-09)
threats: Church and private maintainers, protected by partial exemption from state maintenance jurisdiction, have a strong legal and political basis to contest any extension of ministerial discretion over their admissions decisions as an infringement on institutional autonomy (F-live-legal_and_governance-07)

Enforceability and legitimacy depend on consistent application of a segregation-impact test; without clear statutory criteria, discretionary gatekeeping risks being characterized as arbitrary, especially given the pre-existing tension between centralized control and constitutionally protected institutional autonomy (F-live-legal_and_governance-10)
weaknesses: Extending ministerial discretion over admissions/expansion decisions to church and private maintainers is a materially different and larger legal step than unifying employment terms, and the current jurisdictional carve-out for these maintainers under Klebelsberg Centre creates a direct autonomy conflict not resolved by the employment-law precedent alone (F-live-legal_and_governance-07, F-live-legal_and_governance-09)
